skill.name: dotnet-backend-patterns
skill.description: Guides C# and .NET backend design with examples for APIs, repositories, Entity Framework Core, Dapper, dependency injection, caching, configuration, testing, and resilience.
skill.summary: Build production-oriented C# and .NET backend services with proven API, data access, caching, and testing patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but contextual review found them to be false positives from Markdown fences, C# identifiers, and .NET documentation text. No malicious intent, prompt injection, exfiltration, real command execution, or weak cryptographic implementation was found. The remaining concern is low: one copyable development connection string and one clearly marked unsafe SQL anti-pattern require reader care.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alerts Match C# Documentation Terms
low_findings.description: Static high-risk weak cryptography alerts were reviewed and dismissed. The cited lines contain C# repository and specification examples, JSON serialization examples, or skill description text; no weak cryptographic API or hashing algorithm use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations were inspected in context and contain C# identifiers such as Includes, IncludeStrings, override, and JsonSerializer.Deserialize. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command alerts are Markdown backticks around C# and project-structure examples. They do not execute commands, spawn processes, or instruct the assistant to run shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is fenced Markdown documentation and local resource references. No Ruby backtick execution, command substitution, child process API, or shell invocation appears in those locations.

low_findings.title: Copyable Connection String Uses Development-Only Defaults
low_findings.description: A Dapper connection pooling example includes a placeholder SQL Server password and TrustServerCertificate=True. This is not malicious, but users could copy insecure TLS and credential handling into production if they miss the example context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The connection string is clearly documentation with a placeholder password, which lowers severity. The production risk is credible because TrustServerCertificate=True and inline credentials are copyable defaults.

low_findings.title: Instructional SQL Injection Anti-Pattern Is Clearly Marked Bad
low_findings.description: The Dapper guide shows string-interpolated SQL using user input, but labels it as a bad pattern and immediately provides a parameterized query. This is educational rather than malicious.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The unsafe SQL example is directly marked as an injection risk and paired with the safe alternative. The remaining risk is copy-paste misuse, not malicious skill behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable Connection String Uses Development-Only Defaults
dangerous_patterns.description: A Dapper connection pooling example includes a placeholder SQL Server password and TrustServerCertificate=True. This is not malicious, but users could copy insecure TLS and credential handling into production if they miss the example context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The connection string is clearly documentation with a placeholder password, which lowers severity. The production risk is credible because TrustServerCertificate=True and inline credentials are copyable defaults.

dangerous_patterns.title: Instructional SQL Injection Anti-Pattern Is Clearly Marked Bad
dangerous_patterns.description: The Dapper guide shows string-interpolated SQL using user input, but labels it as a bad pattern and immediately provides a parameterized query. This is educational rather than malicious.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The unsafe SQL example is directly marked as an injection risk and paired with the safe alternative. The remaining risk is copy-paste misuse, not malicious skill behavior.

content.value_statement: C# backend teams need consistent patterns for APIs, data access, caching, testing, and service design. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical .NET examples and review guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains clean architecture structure for C# backend services.
Provides repository examples using Entity Framework Core and Dapper.
Guides dependency injection, options configuration, and service lifetimes.
Covers async, cancellation tokens, caching, and resilience patterns.
Includes unit and integration testing patterns with xUnit-style examples.
Highlights common performance problems such as N+1 queries and over-fetching.
limitations: It is guidance and templates, not a runnable .NET application.
Examples require adaptation to each database schema and domain model.
It does not replace security review for production authentication or authorization.
It focuses on backend .NET and does not cover frontend implementation.

use_cases.title: Design a New API Service
use_cases.description: Plan layers, services, repositories, caching, validation, and tests before building an ASP.NET Core API.

use_cases.title: Review Data Access Code
use_cases.description: Check EF Core and Dapper code for query performance, parameterization, pagination, and transaction boundaries.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Patterns
use_cases.description: Create consistent backend conventions for dependency injection, configuration, error handling, and test coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to explain how a .NET backend should organize controllers, services, repositories, configuration, and tests.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a new ASP.NET Core product API with validation, caching, EF Core or Dapper data access, and xUnit tests.

prompt_templates.title: Review Existing Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my C# repository and service code for async usage, dependency injection, query performance, and test gaps.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a Hot Path
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to propose a Dapper or EF Core optimization plan for a high-traffic read endpoint with caching and cancellation support.

output_examples.input: Design a .NET catalog API with caching and database access.
output: A layered service outline with API, application, infrastructure, and domain responsibilities.
Repository guidance comparing EF Core and Dapper for the expected query workload.
Caching, validation, cancellation, and test recommendations for the main flows.

output_examples.input: Review this Dapper search method for safety and performance.
output: A review focused on parameterized SQL, pagination bounds, query shape, and connection lifetime.
Concrete fixes for unsafe filtering, large result sets, missing cancellation, or repeated queries.
A short test checklist covering empty filters, paging limits, and invalid input.

output_examples.input: Improve EF Core queries in a production endpoint.
output: Recommendations for projections, AsNoTracking, eager loading, split queries, and compiled queries.
Warnings for N+1 queries, over-fetching, lazy loading, and unnecessary tracking.
A migration-safe plan to measure results before and after changes.

best_practices: Use the templates as review guides, then adapt names, schemas, and lifetimes to the real service.
Prefer parameterized queries, bounded pagination, cancellation tokens, and structured logging in copied examples.
Pair implementation changes with focused unit or integration tests for the affected backend flow.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder connection strings or development TLS settings into production.
Do not expose EF entities directly from public APIs without DTO review.
Do not use the skill for non-.NET systems or frontend-only work.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete application?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns, examples, and review guidance that you adapt to your own .NET project.

faq.question: Which data access tools does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Entity Framework Core for domain models and Dapper for performance-focused SQL access.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Does it include testing guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for unit tests, integration tests, validation, and important backend flows.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found false positives in static alerts and only low documentation risks.

faq.question: What should users review before production use?
faq.answer: Users should review connection strings, TLS settings, authentication, authorization, and database-specific behavior.
